results.title: Looking for brutal feedback on my open-source AI Company OS
results.content:
- "What would an operating system for an AI-native company look like?" Linux with added packages.
- We advise startup companies. Working with a few dozen. We are building an internal OS for our business. We're planning on making the rolls we're building available to the companies we work with. We still find the problem ridiculously difficult that we're not sure if we should try to do it as a single OS. Although to be honest, at that point it will make more sense for us, to just implement ourselves as a company factory than try to implement an OS. What I'm saying though is that either you are too optimistic to realize how hard a problem you're talking about or just the level of optimism needed to deny the 100 reasons it won't work and make it possible. [...] # Looking for brutal feedback on my open-source AI Company OS

## Post details
r/AI_Agents | 0 upvotes | 28 comments | Posted by u/aldi949 on 2026-07-17T09:57:37.621Z

## Content
Hi everyone. I'm 17 years old and I've spent the last several months building something I've been thinking about for a long time. Instead of treating AI as another chatbot, I wanted to explore a different question: What would an operating system for an AI-native company look like? A system where AI can reason about projects, decisions, workflows and long-term company memory. I'm not looking for promotion. I'm not asking for GitHub stars. I'm genuinely looking for engineers who can tell me why this idea is wrong or what I'm missing. If people are interested, I'll put the repository in the comments. [...] genuinely miserable) without AI. If that succeeds, the OS emerges naturally. Not the other way around. Also, remember you’re 17. That means you can afford to be wrong repeatedly, which is an enormous advantage if you use it well. Don’t protect the idea—try to break it. If it survives that process, it’ll be much stronger. I’d definitely be interested in seeing the repository. The architecture, constraints, and failure cases matter far more than the vision statement.

results.title: Why I'm Building the Operating System for the AI-Native Enterprise / Sycamore
results.content:
# Why I'm Building the Operating System for the AI-Native Enterprise The enterprise software model is fundamentally broken. Software decays, SaaS is bloated, consultants create dependency. The next generation of enterprise software will be autonomous, continuously learning, and adaptive, but every enterprise system today is built for humans doing the work. AI agents can now reason, act, and learn, and every enterprise I talk to is racing to deploy them. The problem is that the platforms beneath those agents were never designed to support the enterprise. After twenty years of building and scaling platforms at Sun, VMware, Groupon, and Atlassian, I've seen these issues up close. On the investing side at Coatue, I saw the size of the agent readiness problem over and over again. That's why I [...] Skip to content  

Why I'm Building the Operating System for the AI-Native Enterprise

← Blog

Company

# Why I’m Building the Operating System for the AI-Native Enterprise

Sri Viswanath

  Sri Viswanath

The enterprise software model is fundamentally broken. Software decays, SaaS is bloated, consultants create dependency. The next generation of enterprise software will be autonomous, continuously learning, and adaptive, but every enterprise system today is built for humans doing the work. AI agents can now reason, act, and learn, and every enterprise I talk to is racing to deploy them. The problem is that the platforms beneath those agents were never designed to support the enterprise. [...] agent readiness problem over and over again. That's why I started Sycamore: to build the operating system for the AI-native enterprise, where software finally builds itself. ## Patterns: What I Keep Seeing Three patterns keep compounding across the enterprise. Software decays. Teams end up spending more time maintaining what they shipped last year than building what they need next year. The moment software is deployed, it becomes brittle and stale, bugs linger, and integrations drift out of sync with how people actually work. I've seen firsthand how even the most sophisticated engineering organizations struggled to keep their software ecosystems healthy. We've all accepted this decay as the cost of doing business, but I believe it doesn't have to be that way. SaaS has become bloated. The

results.title: The AI-Native OS: Rethinking the Operating System from First Principles
results.content:
✅ Who benefits: Developers, startup founders, hobbyists.

## 10. Time-Aware Contextual Memory

The OS tracks temporal context:

   What were you doing before a break?
   What changed in your project since last week?
   What trends are emerging across your work?

It connects past, present, and future interactions into a coherent timeline.

✅ Who benefits: High-context professionals, creatives, and multi-project individuals.

Edit: As this article has grown in popularity. I built an open source version of this project. Feel free to check it out here: 

Operating Systems

Ai Operating System

Windows

Mac

AI





60K 1388 590

[](

[]( [...] The OS becomes a personal tutor and adaptive coach.

   Tracks your pace and confusion.
   Offers interactive lessons tailored to your projects.
   Integrates learning directly into your workflow (“You’re writing SQL — want a quick refresher on joins?”)

✅ Who benefits: Students, upskillers, career switchers.

## 6. Goal-Oriented Planning

No more opening calendar, task manager, and to-do list separately.

   You say: “Help me prepare for the conference next month.”
   The OS builds a timeline, blocks focused hours, suggests materials, and even preps your slides.

✅ Who benefits: Project managers, consultants, entrepreneurs.

## 7. Life Management and Context Recall

An AI-native OS becomes your external brain. [...] > _“Welcome back. You paused your design flow yesterday — shall I resume where you left off? I also synced your research notes and generated a draft outline based on your highlights.”_

You can type, speak, or gesture. The interface responds to intent, not rigid commands. Visual elements — like memory clusters, AI collaborators, or real-time semantic tools — emerge fluidly when needed. Not as static apps, but as capabilities that adapt to your context.

Need to analyze data? A visual notebook opens with pre-loaded datasets and recommended models.

Want to prototype an idea? A dynamic canvas appears, co-edited by your design agent.

Trying to remember something? Just ask. The OS recalls knowledge across time, apps, and projects — semantically.

The hierarchy breakdown piece is the one that deserves more attention. When an intelligence layer removes the human middleware, the coordination and sense-making roles that managers played don't disappear. They have to be absorbed somewhere. The founders who build AI-native companies well will still need to answer the same questions about clarity, accountability, and decision rights. The org chart changes. The leadership questions don't. This is the bigger shift many people still underestimate. AI is not just changing productivity, it is changing how workflows, decisions, and entire companies can be structured. The real advantage will go to teams that build with AI as operating infrastructure from day one, not as an add-on later.

ve.ai - the intent company   2d 

 Report this comment [...] This framing misses the shift we're currently seeing, which is less about productivity boots than entirely in new capabilities. The right person with AI tools can now build features that used to require an entire team, or we're just impossible thinking. File AI, in terms of new capabilities, has several implications for how founders should run their companies. At a high level, the way to think about AI is that it should not be a tool your company just uses. It should be the operating system your company runs on. Every workflow, every decision, and every process should flow through an intelligent layer that is constantly learning and improving. What this means concretely is every important process in your company should be captured by an intelligent. Close loop. A close loop captures
